text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic duodenal switch procedure by taking down the gastroesophageal fat pad at the upper part of the stomach. Using an energy device in the right hand, the surgeon cauterizes through small blood vessels attaching the fat pad to the stomach at the gastroesophageal junction. The fat pad is completely transected and removed, providing clear access to the angle of His for sleeve creation.

The surgeon directs attention to the lower stomach, identifying the pylorus and measuring four to six centimeters proximal along the greater curve using a grasper. The assistant elevates the stomach as the surgeon begins transecting the short gastric vessels, continuing towards the angle of His to join the upper part of the stomach where the fat pad was previously removed.

The surgeon completes the transection of the short gastric vessels and inserts a bougie, a sizing tube, into the stomach. Using a stapling device, the surgeon staples alongside the fifty French bougie to create a larger sleeve, characteristic of the duodenal switch procedure. The final staple completes the sleeve portion of the procedure.
The surgeon performs an oversewing of the staple line on the sleeve gastrectomy portion of the duodenal switch, using an inverting technique to conceal the staples on the exterior of the stomach.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to create a window under and above the duodenum, carefully transecting small blood vessels. A blunt device is passed posterior to the duodenum, followed by transection with a stapling device.

The stapler is positioned at the duodenum, and the surgeon proceeds to transect the duodenum, completing this portion of the duodenal switch. The transection is visually confirmed as successful. The next step involves measuring the bowel using a ruler to determine a ten-centimeter segment.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and measures approximately 100 centimeters of bowel in a stair-step fashion, leaving most of the bowel in the right lower quadrant. Clips are placed at the 100-centimeter mark for the lower anastomosis. The surgeon then measures back another 150 centimeters to establish the site for the anastomosis, creating a 250-centimeter alimentary limb, which is rotated into the upper abdomen.

The surgeon rotates the loop of small bowel at the 250-centimeter mark towards the upper abdomen and begins sewing it to the duodenal staple line. A two-layered anastomosis is performed, starting with a posterior layer using an endo-stitch device and absorbable suture in a running fashion, attaching the bowel loop to the duodenum across the staple line and ilium.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to create wide openings in the duodenum and ileum, ensuring they are large enough to prevent post-procedure strictures. A vial suture is then used to hand-sew the posterior inner layer of the anastomosis in a running fashion, followed by the anterior inner layer, taking care not to inadvertently close the opening by catching the back wall.

The surgeon completes the anterior inner layer of the anastomosis by tying the suture to the posterior inner layer. Following this, the final anterior outer layer is sewn in an inverting fashion, concluding the two-layered hand-sewn anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a stapling device to transect the biliopancreatic limb, disconnecting bile and pancreatic enzymes from this section of bowel. A flexible endoscope is passed down the esophagus into the antrum to test the anastomosis and staple line of the gastric sleeve, confirming no air leakage and ensuring an airtight sleeve gastrectomy and duodenal ileostomy.

The surgeon locates the clips at the 100-centimeter mark on the small bowel, indicating the site for reattachment of the biliopancreatic limb. An opening is created in the biliopancreatic limb, which is then attached to the alimentary limb using a double staple technique. The stapler is positioned to create a wide anastomosis between the two limbs, ensuring no stricture formation. The bowel is rotated to facilitate a wide opening, and the stapler is fired in opposite directions to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the ilioiliostomy, removing a section of bowel, and closes the mesenteric defect using a permanent suture in a running fashion to prevent herniation. The duodenal ileostomy is then sealed with fibrin sealant, marking the final step of the laparoscopic duodenal switch procedure.

The surgeon removes the gastric specimen through a fifteen millimeter port, using a wound protector to facilitate extraction. The liver retractor is then removed, marking the conclusion of the laparoscopic duodenal switch procedure.
